article_title: JWST Finds Atmospheres Where None Should Exist, Rewriting the Rules for Rocky Exoplanets

content_preview.title: JWST Finds Atmospheres Where None Should Exist, Rewriting the Rules for Rocky Exoplanets
content_preview.summary: Two March discoveries reveal a 10-billion-year-old lava world with a thick atmosphere and a sulfur-dominated planet unlike anything in existing classification systems.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

In the span of a single week, the James Webb Space Telescope has delivered two discoveries that challenge fundamental assumptions about which rocky planets can hold onto atmospheres. On March 16, a team led by the University of Oxford [published findings in *Nature Astronomy*](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41550-026-02815-8) revealing that exoplanet L 98-59 d harbors a sulfur-rich atmosphere above a global magma ocean, representing what researchers call a previously unknown class...

recommendations: 1 source(s) blocked bot access — sources likely valid but not archivable automatically

source_verification.findings: Confirms TOI-561 b dayside temperature of ~3,200°F (1,800°C) vs expected 4,900°F bare rock. Confirms Johanna Teske as lead author. Confirms lower-than-expected density and 'wet lava ball' description. Confirms magma ocean recycling hypothesis.

Confirms Tim Lichtenberg quote: 'It's super old and ultrahot. This planet should not have an atmosphere. And it has one.' Confirms planet is twice as old as our solar system with two Earth masses. Correctly attributed to University of Groningen.

Confirms study published in The Astrophysical Journal Letters led by Carnegie Science astronomer Johanna K. Teske. Confirms orbital period of 10.56 hours, temperature discrepancy, and heat redistribution evidence.

Nature returned HTTP 303 redirect. However, the ScienceDaily coverage of L 98-59 d references this paper and confirms the key claims about sulfur-dominated atmosphere and magma ocean. Article claims are consistent with coverage from other verified sources.

Confirms L 98-59 d is ~35 light-years away orbiting a red dwarf. Confirms detection of sulfur dioxide and hydrogen sulfide in atmosphere. Confirms planet defies traditional classification systems. Confirms 'completely different class of planet dominated by heavy sulfur compounds.'
HTTP 403 (bot blocked). Claims attributed to this source are corroborated by the ScienceDaily article on L 98-59 d covering the same study.

editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All key claims verified against accessible sources. Temperature measurements (3,200°F vs 4,900°F), orbital period (10.56 hours), researcher quotes (Lichtenberg, Teske), planet properties (TOI-561 b mass, L 98-59 d size and distance), and sulfur compound detections all confirmed. Two sources inaccessible but claims cross-verified via other sources covering the same research.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and informative throughout. No sensationalism. Headline uses 'Rewriting the Rules' which is mildly dramatic but supported by the content. The article appropriately notes uncertainties in the 'What We Don't Know' section.
editor_notes.originality: Well-structured piece combining two related JWST discoveries published within a week. The framing that these findings collectively challenge existing planetary classification is well-supported and adds analytical value beyond simple reporting.
recommendations: The Nature Astronomy paper (s41550-026-02815-8) could not be directly verified but claims are well-corroborated by secondary sources.
The Phys.org source blocked bot access; claims attributed to it are corroborated by ScienceDaily coverage of the same study.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Strong science news article covering two significant JWST discoveries. All verifiable claims check out against sources. Appropriate scope for News category at 755 words. Well-balanced structure with clear attribution and an honest accounting of open questions.
